DC1.title: Cognitive & experiential change → willingness to try
DC1.summary: Design should focus on changing picky-eating children's food-related cognition and experiences, so that they become willing to try unfamiliar or disliked foods. Treat willingness-to-try (e.g., approach, smell, lick, tiny bite) as the key outcome rather than completion or quantity.

DC2.title: Non-mealtime, low-pressure narrative intervention + sustainable reinforcement
DC2.summary: Design should primarily intervene in non-mealtime contexts via low-pressure, long-term, playful narrative (e.g., picture-book stories) and provide sustainable daily reinforcement. Respect real-world constraints (eye strain, screen time, timing) and avoid high-operation, disruptive interactions.

content_level_dimensions.name: personalization
content_level_dimensions.definition: Lightweight personalization based on child differences (interests, preferences, target food category/instance) without increasing parent burden.

content_level_dimensions.name: low_barrier_participation
content_level_dimensions.label_cn: 过程参与感重要，低门槛（简单交互）
content_level_dimensions.definition: Support child agency through simple, optional micro-interactions (tap/drag/choice) rather than high-condition participation (e.g., complex cooking/planting).

content_level_dimensions.name: peer_role_model_no_comparison
content_level_dimensions.label_cn: 同伴 / 榜样影响，避免竞争/比较
content_level_dimensions.definition: Use peers/role models to normalize gentle approach and trying, while avoiding competition, ranking, or comparison frames.

content_level_dimensions.name: contextualized_knowledge_age_timing
content_level_dimensions.definition: Embed food knowledge contextually within story and experiences; avoid direct lecturing, and adapt to age and timing constraints.

DC3.title: Lightweight, variable post-meal positive reinforcement without taskification
DC3.summary: Design can use lightweight and varied post-meal positive reinforcement to consolidate cognition and willingness-to-try, while avoiding rigid rule-bound rewards that make eating feel like a task or transaction.

DC4.title: Parent-in-the-loop as co-user and context owner
DC4.summary: Design should involve parents in the system loop because parents and the feeding context strongly shape picky eating. Provide low-burden parent scaffolds and guardrails to support sustainable use without coercion.
